speaker: interviewer
text: Vladka Meed. When you came back to Warsaw after the Warsaw Uprising, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in a small town in Grojec with Ben. And when the action, the Russian offensive, started in January, and we saw the Russians coming, we decided to go back to Warsaw, knowing that Warsaw is destroyed. But nevertheless, we thought that certain people are alive. And we knew the bunker where a few of our Warsaw ghetto fighters were hiding. And we went to Warsaw by foot. We were walking from Grojec to Warsaw. We came-- of course, it was destruction. It was army, and civilian, and certain houses were destroyed and bombed. It was a very sad and very bad situation. But we went, and we met a few of people who were hiding. We kept together in Warsaw, when the Russians came. And little by little, we get to know more. I was sure that we are the only ones-- the only two, or three, or four. But people started to come in from hiding places from the surrounding to Warsaw. And as the front went more and more to the West, more people, Jews, came into this city. But Warsaw was destroyed, and it was very difficult to find a place where to live. So when Lodz, the city which was not destroyed, was liberated, many people went to Lodz. And Ben and I also decided to go to Lodz. In Lodz, a Jewish community started out, and a Jewish committee was organized. And the first people who saw me there turn to me to be the so-called director or manager of the Jewish Cultural Department. So I became the director of the Jewish Cultural Department. And how the Jewish committee looked at that time was a peculiar, really, picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were coming from concentration camps, from the forest, and putting on the walls, on the doors, scraps of paper. Did you see? Did you hear this and this name? Maybe you heard something. Please notify. And although everybody knew that you will not have anybody, nevertheless the walls were covered with paper. And I was walking in the committee and observing this. And suddenly, you can hear, oh, whom I see here? Or, how did you survive? And being part of the tense and nervous atmosphere, knowing that I don't have to look for anybody because the people from Warsaw were sent to Treblinka, and none of my closest ones survived. Nevertheless, I also put a scrap of paper on the wall. Maybe. Maybe my sister, my brother. They were so young. Maybe somewhere they are.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was not lucky. This situation, none of my family survived, absolutely nobody. But I was walking there, and the first I remember that I organized at that time, the first Jewish event of people-- of survivors for survivors. I did it with all my soul, what I still had in me, and Jewish songs. And it is very characteristic that in the camps many Jewish songs were created. In the Vilna ghetto, many songs were created. And at this event, they were singing-- those who had voices. And the reaction, the response of the people who came, was overwhelming. Overwhelming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What songs are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What songs are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were Jewish songs from the ghetto. In the Vilna ghetto for instance, I cannot sing here. Maybe someday, but not now. And the Vilna ghetto, they had theater during deportation time. In the Warsaw ghetto were Polish songs. But people were singing. And also, they were singing Israeli songs. Because at that time, it was the trend and the feeling to go to Israel, to go to your own country, which is not your own yet. It was an atmosphere of survival and, at the same time, of sadness. A realization that you are-- after all, you survived, but you are alone. And this was very difficult to live with. You went on-- you was constantly waiting, maybe, maybe, maybe, hoping and realizing, day by day, time passed, that no, this is the reality. And at the same time, thinking that life goes on. Life goes on. And then, in Poland, it was, again, anti-Semitic incident. People coming from the forest, from two small towns, were robbed, were killed. The Poles, Ukrainians, and other, they didn't want them back. And I remember also before I left Poland, and before I worked in the Jewish committee, that the pogrom in Kielce took place, a Polish city where it was a Jewish community from a small group of survivors. And it was pogrom by-- it was by Poles. And the Polish police, and even at that time, the Russian police did not take care, did not control the situation. And over 40 people were killed at that time, saying that the Jews are taking because of some rumor, the Jews were taking blood from a Polish boy to prepare the matzahs for Pesach for the holiday, for Eastern holiday, which was a rumor later that they killed the boy. The boy was alive. They found the boy, but nevertheless, after the boy said the truth, for the people already were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Polish government at the time that was active, that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a Polish government, but it was under the Soviet-- not occupation, but supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing was done about these Pogroms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, after the people were killed, they controlled the situation. Now, not long ago, they told me that in July, it's going to be a monument put up for the-- over for the killed Jews. But it was a Pogrom, and this was after the German-- there were not anymore German pogrom by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to take you back--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was anti-Semitism in the towns, on trains, and people were afraid to go back to the places and smaller towns. But even a city like Kielce, it was a polgrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to go back for a minute to the time when you came back to Warsaw, the first time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the ghetto. What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't see the ghetto anymore. I just saw rubble. And my first instinct when I came back was to go to the cemetery, and to put up at tomb for my father. Because I knew that he died with a natural death, and he was buried at the cemetery, but I couldn't find his grave. There were skulls, skulls on the ground, and I didn't know which is his. Which is his grave? How can I put up a tomb? And I prepared already this stone. And nothing. I couldn't find the grave. Years, years later, I put down a symbolic tomb for my father. But it's just a symbolic tomb. It's not a place where he really was put.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the graves dug up?

speaker: interviewee
text: The graves were broken. There were Polish grave diggers which took out the skulls, and they took out the teeth, and the gold teeth. And this is the way-- how they were handling the-- It is so difficult to think about, that, you know, even how people can do this to the dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen before or after the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened after the Germans left. Because the Germans would not tolerate that somebody will do these things. They will be afraid. But after the German left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you and Ben do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after, we decided to leave Poland. Although, I was working in the Jewish committee, and his parents were united with him. And we decided to leave Poland. We went to Germany meantime. We contacted people in the states. And they sent out visas for us, and we came to the United States on the displaced person visas, which the President Truman assigned. And when we came here, we didn't have family, but we have friends from before the war. And I started that time talking, reminiscences. And they get down to me the Jewish daily, that I should write articles. And I started to write articles. Every week, an article. I wrote about 27 articles. And from the they turned again. If you wrote so many articles, and the writing is fine, write a book. And this, the articles were the base. I started a book when deportation started. I didn't go back from before the war, because this was really the knock. The deportation was the main difference which cut off life. And my book, which I wrote, I wrote by myself in Yiddish. It was later translated into Hebrew, and then into Spanish, and then into English. And then, not long ago, to Japanese. So of all the languages. And it is a little bit, for me, a surprise that now, more and more is being taught about the Holocaust, that in the beginning, when I wrote the book. I remember when I wrote the book, and they wanted to publish this in English, the English publisher said, it's no interest for Holocaust literature at that time. And this was 10 years after the Holocaust. Today, 50 years after, is more awareness what took place than it was right after the Holocaust. And my interest now, when I start reading what people are writing or talking about the Holocaust, is special about armed resistance and about the people went like sheep to the slaughter, was such a resentment that I dedicated a lot of talking how the real situation in the ghetto. How the 1,000 instances of resistance Jews put up, about what so little is known, and not even recognized in today's time, when violence is constantly on the daily agenda. And Ben also-- Ben, my husband, Benjamin-- he also dedicated in organizing commemorations. He organized, each year, a large public commemoration. Once, two years ago, President Clinton came. Vice President Gore then came. Thousands of people are always present. He's also the one who is organizing the commemoration lately in the rotunda. And he is one of the first builders of the Holocaust Memorial Museum in Washington. Our life is very much connected with the Holocaust. It's an inner dedication to a life which is no more, and which very often might think it's not recognized. Lately, you know, I think that we are talking so much about the number of 6 million. About the people who were killing, the perpetrators. And we see the numbers, we see a statistic, but we don't see the life-- very little about the life, the life in the ghetto. The life before the outbreak of the war, the life after. The continuation of life, and what life the survivors built later on. Two children were born in the United States. Both are part of our life. We spoke with the children in Yiddish till they started school. Not because we were not able to speak English. We want to preserve something from the past in a direct way. We send them to a Yiddish school, and it did not disturb them to become physicians, doctors, both of them. They speak, they write Yiddish. My daughter sings Yiddish, even-- songs. And my son taught Jewish history while going to medical school. They both are physicians. In it, I have a feeling it's a continuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this kind of continuation, I constantly would like to have it seen among Jews. And I would like not the Jewish population-- the American, or not American, the other religious-- to know what kind of life was destroyed. Not only people, but what kind of values were destroyed. What have become of those people who have lived? And this is important. This is why I organized a teachers' seminar of public school teachers from high schools who are implementing the history of the Holocaust and Jewish resistance, as I understand. Which, the culmination was organized and resistant. But it's a chain of resistance. And the seminar which I organized, the three weeks in Israel, and now with a stop in Poland, after the fall of Jaruzelski's government, is in a way satisfaction. How did people see the Holocaust with the eyes of a survivor who was part of the life which is no more? Does the preservation of this Jewishness have anything to do with religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it has to do. Jewish history is constantly connected in part of religion. Of course. It has to do with religion. It has to do with tradition. It has to do with moral, with ethic. It has to do with a philosophy of life, which has to be understood by non-Jews too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell us. When did you get married? When did you and Ben get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't tell you. It was really a historical time. It was right after the war, right after. It was still-- the Russians were still in Warsaw. And the parents, when we came back to have to go back, when we came back to Warsaw, the parents came from their hiding. And his uncle also was there. He came from hiding. And they start talking to me. You work together? You have to have a Jewish marriage. And it was the first, I think, Jewish marriage-- not in Warsaw, but in the suburb of Prague. And at that time, I have to get something white. And I didn't have anything. It's nothing to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got-- I bought, on the market, a white blouse. And this was my white dress. And there were then Jews. It was very primitive, very modest, but it was an official Jewish wedding, yes. Was there a chuppah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. It was a chuppah. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somebody who gave chuppah kiddushin. It was not a formal rabbi, but somebody who had the right to give us chuppah kiddushin. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a dinner after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother-in-law, Ben's mother, she prepared a very nice soup. It was not the festive dinners we do in America, in the states, the wedding. But it was a festive occasion. Yes, it was a festive, formal so-called occasion. And it was a real wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to the United States, did Ben's parents also come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We came to the United States. We got the visas. But before our leaving Germany, Bremen. Ben's parents went to Israel. Before. They were able to go before. And we were waiting to go. And in the meantime, the visas came from the displaced person visas, and we came to the States. So the parents were Israeli, and we were in the States. But we were in constant communication. We went there in 1949, a year after the establishment of Israel. We went there, and at that time, we have already one-year-old daughter. And it was really a joy when they were playing with them. It is-- you know, when I come to Israel, it is like a continuation of Jewish revival. And it's something always very close to me. Although I was never a Zionist, political, but I got very close to the Jewish people after the Holocaust. Very close. It's part of me. And wherever a Jew lives-- it can live somewhere, in Japan or something, I feel it's part of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have how many children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have my daughter. She is a doctor, medicine. She has two children and lives in Phoenix, Arizona. And our son, he's internal medicine, rheumatology. He lives in New York, and he has three children. So we have five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jessica is my oldest grandchild, our oldest grandchild. She's 17 years old, and just told me that she made Dartmouth, Ivy League college. And she's going to attend. Chavala is 13 years old. And the little one is Jonathan [INAUDIBLE], Jonathan. He's five years old. And my daughter's children is Jenny, 16 years old, and Michael, 14 years old. So together, five children, grandchildren. I'm very proud of them. They very much know my past. And I was-- you know, I took pride when I went into the room of my granddaughter, the oldest one. And above her bed was my false identification card, which is in the Washington Museum placed. She copied-- she put it in a frame over her bed. This is my son's daughter, and mine. I have to laugh. My grandson, Michael, when it was the question about-- to mention in history class about what of the women you remember and you would like to talk about who showed some specific heroism. And this boy said, my grandmother. So the teacher ask him, how can you show us that she really-- she wrote a book. So OK. Then you have to tell us about the book. You have to read the book and give us a report.

speaker: interviewer
text: He called me up from Phoenix and said, Bubbe, I have to make a report about the book. Can you tell me what? I said, no, you have to read it. He read 100 pages and called me again. I read already 100. Can you give me the end? I said, no, you have to finish it off. So he had to finish the book, and it is-- he finished the book, and he told me that he got an A-plus. Because he had a report, and he said everything what the teacher expected. And this is how he learned about my past. I never talk with them too much about it. It is in a natural way they know. Did you ever get in touch with Anna?

speaker: interviewee
text: What Anna?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna who gave you the papers, who was your mother in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Anna Wonchalska? Oh, I have history with Anna Wonchalska. Anna Wonchalska remembered always the birthday of my son and my daughter. And she used to send me all kind of books or sausages. And one time, I got a sausage, a package from her with a sausage so small, it took about two months till it came to us. And in the meantime, it dried up from a big sausage and it became a very small one. And she sent, to my son, [NON-ENGLISH] from Korczak. Janusz Korczak. This is a Polish writer, who wrote especially for children. And I read it to my son in English, of course, translating. And he knows-- he still has it. He put it away. So Anna knew about our children, and she was sending gifts, and I was sending to her gifts, medication. We had-- we were in contact till she died. And I am still in contact with her sister, with [NON-ENGLISH]. She lives in Lodz. And whenever I am in Poland, I am contact-- and of course, I help her. I sent some money. So I am in contact with them, of course. Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Anna have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was the only daughter whose identification card, the birth certificate, I had. She gave me. She had one daughter who died now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know Korczak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just from reading. And just one time, while it was during deportation, I was in the house of one of our hiding places, and I saw through the window how, at that time, all the orphanages were deported. Not all, but full. And among them was Janusz Korczak. And I saw him marching with his orphanage. And this was August the 5th, I think. And at that time, I saw him form the fourth floor looking down. He was going through [NON-ENGLISH]. And all the stooped men and the children looking bewildered around, not knowing exactly where they are going. Maybe he knew where he was going. Because later on, I read that he was suggested and that he was being asked to get out of the Polish side into hiding the people. He was a doctor, but he was also a very famous educator in Poland. And he was a writer, but he didn't want to leave the children. And he went with them to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you want to leave to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are a few messages I want to leave. The first message is, I would like very much the people should know and understand this chapter, this unique chapter of history, the Holocaust. They should understand the Jewish resistance. I would like also to say to people not to take for granted-- not freedom, not justice. You have to stand up for it. You have to defend it. To be your brother's keeper, to remember that the other needs, too, your help. Not to be passive when things are going on around you. Not to be indifferent. To be a participant in life, and to defend the rights of humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your little children? The grandchildren? Anything specific to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is to them just the same as the message to others. They are part of the human society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vladka, I want to thank you for this interview. I am very honored that you told us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, too--

speaker: interviewer
text: And God bless you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for being able to say something about the past, which is part of me. And I hope that it will remain part of other peoples, of human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
